subject: ap-precalculus
theme: Trigonometric and polar functions model periodic phenomena and circular motion, connecting angle measures, the unit circle, and transformable sinusoidal patterns to real-world cycles.

core_concepts.title: Unit Circle and Radian Measure
core_concepts.detail: The unit circle has radius 1 centered at the origin. For angle $\theta$ in standard position, $\cos\theta$ is the $x$-coordinate and $\sin\theta$ is the $y$-coordinate of the terminal point. Key exact values: $\cos\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{1}{2}$, $\sin\frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $\sin\frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Radian measure is the arc length on a unit circle: $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$. One full revolution is $2\pi$ radians $= 360^{\circ}$.

core_concepts.title: Sinusoidal Transformations
core_concepts.detail: The function $f(x) = A\sin(B(x - C)) + D$ has: amplitude $|A|$ (half the vertical range), period $\frac{2\pi}{|B|}$, phase shift $C$ (horizontal shift right if $C > 0$), and midline $y = D$. Given a graph, read the max and min to find $A = \frac{\max - \min}{2}$ and $D = \frac{\max + \min}{2}$. The tangent function $\tan(Bx)$ has a shorter period: $T = \frac{\pi}{|B|}$, with vertical asymptotes wherever its denominator ($\cos$) equals zero.

core_concepts.title: Pythagorean Identity
core_concepts.detail: The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ holds for all $\theta$. Dividing through by $\cos^2\theta$ gives $\tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta$; dividing by $\sin^2\theta$ gives $1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta$.

core_concepts.title: Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent
core_concepts.detail: The reciprocal functions are defined as $\sec\theta = \frac{1}{\cos\theta}$, $\csc\theta = \frac{1}{\sin\theta}$, and $\cot\theta = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta}$. Their domains exclude values where the denominator equals zero: $\sec$ and $\tan$ are undefined where $\cos\theta = 0$ (i.e., $\theta = \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$); $\csc$ and $\cot$ are undefined where $\sin\theta = 0$ (i.e., $\theta = n\pi$). Vertical asymptotes of $y = \sec x$ occur at $x = \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$; $\sec x \geq 1$ or $\sec x \leq -1$, so the range is $(-\infty, -1] \cup [1, \infty)$. A useful simplification: $\sec\theta \cdot \sin\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \tan\theta$.

core_concepts.title: Angle Sum, Double-Angle, and Half-Angle Identities
core_concepts.detail: These identities (CED Topic 3.12) rewrite trig expressions in equivalent forms. Angle sum: $\sin(\alpha \pm \beta) = \sin\alpha\cos\beta \pm \cos\alpha\sin\beta$; $\cos(\alpha \pm \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta \mp \sin\alpha\sin\beta$. Double angle: $\sin(2\alpha) = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$; $\cos(2\alpha)$ has three equivalent forms — use $\cos^2\alpha - \sin^2\alpha$ when both functions are present, use $2\cos^2\alpha - 1$ when you want to eliminate $\sin$, and use $1 - 2\sin^2\alpha$ when you want to eliminate $\cos$. Half-angle (power-reduction): $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}$; $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$ — these are essential for solving equations with $\sin^2$ or $\cos^2$ terms.

core_concepts.title: Inverse Trig Restrictions and Composition
core_concepts.detail: Inverse trig functions require restricted domains to be functions: $\sin^{-1}$ has range $\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$, $\cos^{-1}$ has range $[0, \pi]$, and $\tan^{-1}$ has range $\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Always report the principal value. For compositions, use right-triangle reasoning: if $\theta = \arcsin(x)$, draw a right triangle with opposite side $x$ and hypotenuse $1$, so the adjacent side is $\sqrt{1-x^2}$. This gives $\cos(\arcsin(x)) = \sqrt{1-x^2}$ (positive because $\arcsin$ returns angles in $\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]$ where cosine $\geq 0$). Similarly, $\sin(\arccos(x)) = \sqrt{1-x^2}$.

core_concepts.title: Trigonometric Inequalities
core_concepts.detail: To solve an inequality like $\sin(x) > c$ on $[0, 2\pi]$: (1) find the reference angles where $\sin(x) = c$ using $\arcsin$; (2) sketch the unit circle or sine graph to identify which portion of the circle has $y$-coordinate $> c$; (3) write the solution as a union of intervals. For example, $\sin(x) > \frac{1}{2}$ on $[0, 2\pi]$: the equation $\sin(x) = \frac{1}{2}$ has solutions $x = \frac{\pi}{6}$ and $x = \frac{5\pi}{6}$; since sine is above $\frac{1}{2}$ between these values, the solution set is $\left(\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5\pi}{6}\right)$. For $\cos$ inequalities, identify the arc where the $x$-coordinate satisfies the inequality.

core_concepts.title: Polar Coordinates and Curve Behavior
core_concepts.detail: Polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ locate a point by distance $r$ from the origin and angle $\theta$. Converting: $x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$; reverse: $r = \sqrt{x^2+y^2}$, $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$ (adjust for quadrant). When $r = 0$ the curve passes through the origin (the pole). When $r < 0$, the point is plotted in the direction opposite to $\theta$ — it reflects through the pole. To identify a polar curve type, build a table of $(r, \theta)$ values: if $r$ is constant, it is a circle; if $r = a\cos(n\theta)$ or $r = a\sin(n\theta)$, it is a rose ($n$ petals for odd $n$, $2n$ petals for even $n$); if $r = a + b\cos\theta$, it is a limaçon (cardioid when $|a|=|b|$, inner loop when $|b|>|a|$). Negative $r$ values in the table signal the inner loop.

formulas.name: Sinusoidal Function Form
formulas.description: The general form of a transformed sine (or cosine) function. A is the amplitude, B controls the period (T = 2π/|B|), C is the phase shift right, and D is the midline (vertical shift).
formulas.example: $f(x) = 3\sin\!\left(2\left(x - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right) + 1$ has amplitude $3$, period $\frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$, phase shift $\frac{\pi}{4}$ right, and midline $y = 1$.

formulas.name: Period Formula (Sine and Cosine)
formulas.description: The period is the horizontal distance for one complete cycle of a sinusoidal function. A larger |B| compresses the graph horizontally, giving a shorter period; smaller |B| stretches it.
formulas.example: For $f(x) = \cos(3x)$, the period is $T = \frac{2\pi}{3}$, so the graph completes a full cycle every $\frac{2\pi}{3}$ units.

formulas.name: Period Formula (Tangent)
formulas.description: Tangent completes one full cycle in half the distance of sine or cosine because it repeats after every 180°. The period of tan(Bx) is π/|B|, not 2π/|B|.
formulas.example: For $f(x) = \tan(2x)$, the period is $T = \frac{\pi}{2}$. Vertical asymptotes occur at $x = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{n\pi}{2}$ for integer $n$, so consecutive asymptotes are $\frac{\pi}{2}$ apart (one full period).

formulas.name: Radian–Degree Conversion
formulas.description: Multiply degrees by π/180 to get radians; multiply radians by 180/π to get degrees. Radian measure is the standard on the AP Precalculus exam.

formulas.name: Pythagorean Identity
formulas.description: The fundamental trigonometric identity, valid for all angles. It follows directly from the unit circle definition and is the starting point for simplifying trig expressions and solving trig equations.
formulas.example: If $\sin\theta = \frac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant II, then $\cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{9}{25} = \frac{16}{25}$, so $\cos\theta = -\frac{4}{5}$.

formulas.name: Reciprocal Trig Function Definitions
formulas.description: Secant is the reciprocal of cosine, cosecant is the reciprocal of sine, and cotangent is cosine over sine. These are undefined wherever their denominators equal zero, creating vertical asymptotes.

formulas.name: Angle Sum / Difference — Sine
formulas.description: The sine of a sum or difference of two angles. The sign on the right matches the sign in the argument: sin(α + β) uses +, sin(α − β) uses −. Use this to find exact values for non-standard angles or to simplify expressions.

formulas.name: Angle Sum / Difference — Cosine
formulas.description: The cosine of a sum or difference. Note the sign flip: the ∓ means cos(α + β) uses minus and cos(α − β) uses plus — opposite to the signs in the argument.

formulas.name: Double-Angle Identities
formulas.description: Special cases of the angle sum formulas with α = β. The three cosine forms are all equivalent (use the Pythagorean identity to convert); choose whichever form matches the given expression.

formulas.name: Half-Angle (Power-Reduction) Identities
formulas.description: Derived by solving the double-angle cosine forms for sin²θ and cos²θ. These reduce squared trig functions to single-angle cosine expressions, which is essential for solving trig equations and verifying identities.
formulas.example: Solve $2\sin^2\theta = 1$: rewrite as $2\cdot\frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} = 1$, so $1 - \cos(2\theta) = 1$, giving $\cos(2\theta) = 0$, thus $2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$, so $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{n\pi}{2}$.

formulas.name: Polar to Rectangular
formulas.description: Converts a polar point (r, θ) to rectangular coordinates (x, y). The reverse uses r² = x² + y² and θ = arctan(y/x), adjusting for the correct quadrant.
formulas.example: The polar point $\left(4, \frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ converts to $x = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = 2$ and $y = 4 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 2\sqrt{3}$.

formulas.name: Rectangular to Polar
formulas.description: Converts rectangular coordinates (x, y) to polar form (r, θ). Always check the quadrant of (x, y) to determine the correct angle, since arctan returns values only in (−π/2, π/2).
formulas.example: The point $(-1, \sqrt{3})$ has $r = \sqrt{1+3} = 2$ and $\theta = \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ (Quadrant II adjustment).

formulas.name: Key Unit Circle Values
formulas.description: The four key sine values in the first quadrant. Cosine values are the reverse: cos(π/6) = √3/2, cos(π/4) = √2/2, cos(π/3) = 1/2. These must be memorized for rapid evaluation.

exam_tip: When reading a sinusoidal graph, identify the maximum and minimum values first: amplitude $= \frac{\text{max} - \text{min}}{2}$ and midline $= \frac{\text{max} + \text{min}}{2}$, letting you extract $A$ and $D$ without algebra. Watch for the #1 phase-shift error: $f(x) = A\sin(Bx - C)$ is NOT the same factoring as $A\sin(B(x - C))$ — in the first form the phase shift is $\frac{C}{B}$, while in the second form it is simply $C$; always factor out $B$ first before reading the shift.
